description: Figure 1 presents a schematic temperature-time profile illustrating the rolling and annealing schedule for the experimental Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel. The black solid curve traces the air-cooling (AC) path, whereas the red dashed curve traces the furnace-cooling (FC) path. The process begins with reheating at 1200 °C for 2 h, followed by hot rolling within the 900-1100 °C range (visualized as a sawtooth-like temperature decline), after which the sheet is cooled to ambient temperature either by air cooling or furnace cooling. Both routes then proceed to an annealing step and subsequent final cooling. The caption states that the rolled sheets, approximately 4 mm thick after seven passes, were annealed for 30 min at 800, 900, and 1000 °C, yielding sample designations AC-A800/900/1000 and FC-A800/900/1000. The pronounced difference in cooling rate between the steep air-cooling segment and the gradual furnace-cooling segment highlights the primary process variable. This schematic underpins the central finding that the post-hot-rolling cooling path critically governs the evolved microstructure and the resulting strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents a comparative microstructural characterization of hot-rolled Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density duplex steel processed via air cooling (AC, left column) and furnace cooling (FC, right column). Panels (a) and (b) are SEM micrographs revealing banded morphologies along the rolling direction, where alternating δ-ferrite layers and prior austenite regions are visible; panel (a) additionally labels a γ+α* region, while panel (b) labels δ and γ phases. Panels (c) and (d) are EBSD-based KAM (Kernel Average Misorientation) maps overlaid on IQ (Image Quality) maps of austenite, with a top color scale from 0° to 4° indicating local misorientation; black lines denote high-angle grain boundaries and phase boundaries (>15°) and red lines denote low-angle grain boundaries (>2°), as defined in the caption. The AC sample exhibits a darker IQ signature and higher KAM values, consistent with the text-reported higher martensite fraction (26 vol% versus 2.8 vol%) and greater geometrically necessary dislocation density (KAM 0.48° versus 0.43°) due to the faster cooling rate, whereas the FC sample retains a higher austenite fraction (51 vol% versus 42%). Both conditions display banded δ-ferrite containing low-angle boundaries. The figure establishes that the post-rolling cooling path critically controls the as-hot-rolled phase constitution, martensite content, and internal stress state, which govern subsequent annealing behavior and the strength-ductility synergy.

Figure 3 presents SEM micrographs illustrating the annealed microstructures of an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel processed via two distinct cooling paths after hot rolling. Panels (a–c) show air-cooled (AC) samples annealed at 800, 900, and 1000 °C (AC-A800, AC-A900, AC-A1000), respectively, while panels (d–f) display furnace-cooled (FC) counterparts (FC-A800, FC-A900, FC-A1000). The AC-annealed microstructures consist of austenite (γ) and needle-like α-ferrite embedded within the austenite bands, alongside δ-ferrite, with progressively coarser austenite grains as annealing temperature increases. In contrast, FC-annealed samples exhibit a banded morphology comprising δ-ferrite, prior austenite, and a small fraction of martensite (α′), plus fine equiaxed α-ferrite, but notably lack needle-like α-ferrite. The average austenite grain sizes in FC samples are substantially larger than those in AC samples at each temperature. These microstructural differences demonstrate that the initial cooling path critically governs phase morphology, grain refinement, and austenite stability, directly influencing the subsequent strength-ductility synergy.

Figure 4 presents EBSD-based KAM maps overlaid on IQ maps for an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel, comparing air-cooled (AC, panels a–c) and furnace-cooled (FC, panels d–f) samples annealed at 800, 900, and 1000 °C. Red lines denote low-angle grain boundaries (>2°) and black lines denote high-angle grain boundaries (>15°), with a 50 μm scale bar in each panel. Visually, the AC samples exhibit needle-like α-ferrite embedded within austenite bands and dense low-angle grain boundaries in δ-ferrite that progressively diminish with increasing annealing temperature. The caption and text report corresponding low-angle boundary lengths of 4351.1 μm, 2685.4 μm, and 1634.3 μm, and average austenite grain sizes of 3.5 μm, 4.2 μm, and 5.8 μm for AC-A800, AC-A900, and AC-A1000, respectively. In contrast, the FC samples retain a pronounced banded morphology of δ-ferrite and prior austenite without needle-like α-ferrite, accompanied by darker IQ contrast indicating higher lattice distortion; the text reports coarser austenite grain sizes of 9.3 μm, 8.1 μm, and 10.1 μm. These microstructural differences confirm that air cooling produces a refined, recovered duplex structure with stabilized austenite, whereas furnace cooling yields banded coarse grains containing martensite, which governs the observed divergence in strength-ductility performance.

This grouped bar chart compares the austenite volume fraction (vol.%) measured by XRD in an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel before and after tensile deformation. The x-axis displays six sample conditions: air-cooled (AC) and furnace-cooled (FC) samples annealed at 800, 900, and 1000 °C, designated AC800, AC900, AC1000, FC800, FC900, and FC1000. Red solid bars represent austenite fractions before deformation, while gray hatched bars show fractions after deformation. Before deformation, all samples contain similar austenite levels ranging from 57.1% to 62%. After deformation, the air-cooled samples exhibit substantial reductions—AC800 drops from 58.6% to 23.7%, AC900 from 61.4% to 44.4%, and AC1000 from 57.1% to 46.4%—indicating extensive strain-induced martensitic transformation. Conversely, furnace-cooled samples show only minor decreases (FC800: 62% to 56.5%; FC900: 58.6% to 52.4%; FC1000: 60.6% to 52.1%). This marked contrast demonstrates that air-cooled annealed samples possess lower mechanical stability of austenite, enabling a pronounced transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) effect that contributes to work hardening rate recovery and enhanced ductility, whereas the higher austenite stability and presence of brittle martensite in furnace-cooled samples suppresses the TRIP effect.

Figure 6 presents two SEM micrographs showing the microstructural evolution in an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel that was air-cooled after hot rolling and subsequently annealed at 800 °C. Subfigure (a) corresponds to a 5 min annealing treatment; the citation states that this short-time annealed microstructure consists of tempered martensite, austenite, and δ-ferrite, and that the twin crystal structure within the martensite becomes more obvious. Subfigure (b) corresponds to 15 min annealing, revealing that prior martensite has partly degenerated into needle-like α-ferrite. The citation explains that high-density dislocations in twin martensite offer nucleation sites for the ferritic transformation, so the resulting α-ferrite inherits the morphology of the prior martensite. With prolonged annealing, the film austenite located between the needle-like α-ferrite and/or prior martensite becomes thickened, implying an increase in austenite fraction. These images support the conclusion that annealing duration governs the decomposition of prior martensite into needle-like α-ferrite and the reverse transformation to austenite, which refines austenite grains and enhances austenite stability, thereby contributing to the fine-grained duplex microstructure and the good strength-ductility match in air-cooled annealed samples.

Figure 7 presents the normalized area proportion distributions of austenite grain size for an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel after annealing at 800, 900, and 1000 °C following two post-hot-rolling cooling paths. Panel (a) shows air-cooled (AC) annealed samples AC-A800 (black), AC-A900 (red), and AC-A1000 (blue), while panel (b) shows furnace-cooled (FC) annealed samples FC-A800 (black), FC-A900 (red), and FC-A1000 (blue). In both panels, histograms with fitted probability curves reveal that increasing annealing temperature shifts the distribution toward larger grain sizes. Visually, the AC series exhibits relatively narrow distributions peaking at approximately 3–4, 5–6, and 8–9 μm, respectively, whereas the FC series displays broader profiles that are overall shifted to larger sizes. The accompanying text reports that the average austenite grain sizes of AC-A800, AC-A900, and AC-A1000 are 3.5, 4.2, and 5.8 μm, while those of FC-A800, FC-A900, and FC-A1000 are 9.3, 8.1, and 10.1 μm, indicating substantially coarser grains after furnace cooling. This figure supports the core conclusion that air cooling promotes austenite grain refinement via embedded needle-like α-ferrite, thereby enhancing austenite stability and suppressing martensite formation, whereas furnace cooling yields larger, less stable austenite grains that favor martensite formation and degrade ductility.

This schematic illustrates the microstructural evolution during annealing of an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel after hot rolling, comparing air-cooled (AC) and furnace-cooled (FC) samples. The upper row depicts the AC path: the initial structure contains δ-ferrite and γ-austenite with α′-martensite; during annealing, part of the martensite reversely transforms to austenite while the remainder degrades into needle-like α-ferrite embedded within austenite bands, refining austenite grains and stabilizing the phase so that the final microstructure consists of δ-ferrite, austenite, and needle-like α-ferrite without martensite after final cooling. The lower row depicts the FC path: the initial structure contains δ-ferrite and austenite; during annealing, austenite enlarges and slight fine equiaxed α-ferrite forms, but the lower stability of coarse austenite causes partial transformation to α′-martensite upon final cooling, yielding δ-ferrite, austenite, equiaxed α-ferrite, and martensite. The figure demonstrates that the prior cooling path controls austenite stability and final annealed phases, thereby governing the strength-ductility synergy.

Figure 9 presents tensile engineering stress–strain curves for an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel subjected to air cooling (a) and furnace cooling (b) after hot rolling, with subsequent annealing at 800 °C, 900 °C, and 1000 °C. In panel (a), the as-hot-rolled air-cooled sample (AC) exhibits ultrahigh strength but limited ductility. The air-cooled annealed samples (AC800, AC900, AC1000) show a superior strength–ductility combination with pronounced work hardening and elongations above 30%; caption reports values of 56.9%, 39.5%, and 31.1%, respectively. In panel (b), the furnace-cooled samples (FC, FC800, FC900, FC1000) all fracture abruptly at low strains without necking, with elongations below 20% (19.0%, 15.4%, and 10.6% for annealed variants). The contrast demonstrates that air cooling promotes a refined austenite–ferrite duplex microstructure and transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) effect, yielding extra work hardening rate recovery, whereas furnace cooling produces banded ferrite–austenite–martensite microstructures prone to interfacial cracking, severely degrading ductility.

Figure 10 presents the tensile true stress–strain curves and corresponding work hardening rate curves for an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel after different cooling and annealing treatments. Subfigure (a) shows air-cooled (AC) samples annealed at 800 °C (AC800, red), 900 °C (AC900, blue), and 1000 °C (AC1000, green), while subfigure (b) shows furnace-cooled (FC) samples annealed at identical temperatures (FC800, FC900, FC1000). For all curves, the lower traces represent true stress versus true strain, and the upper traces represent work hardening rate versus true strain. The AC samples exhibit three distinct stages: a rapid drop in Stage I, a gradual decrease in Stage II, and an extra Stage III where the work hardening rate recovers—most prominently for AC1000—before final fracture at strains exceeding 0.25 (up to ~0.44 for AC800). In contrast, the FC samples only display Stages I and II, followed by abrupt fracture without necking at much lower strains (below 0.19). The elongations of AC samples are all above 30%, whereas FC samples remain below 20%. This figure demonstrates that air cooling after hot rolling promotes a duplex ferrite-austenite microstructure that sustains high strain levels via transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP), whereas furnace cooling leads to banded ferrite-austenite-martensite microstructures that trigger premature cracking and inferior strength-ductility combinations.

Figure 11 presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images comparing fracture surface features of an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel after different post-hot-rolling cooling paths and annealing at 800 °C, with a 50 μm scale bar in each subfigure. Subfigure (a) shows the air-cooled sample AC-A800, characterized by numerous dimples indicative of ductile fracture, along with localized cracking. Subfigure (b) shows the furnace-cooled sample FC-A800, exhibiting extensive cleavage facets and multiple cracks aligned with the banded microstructure, consistent with brittle fracture. The stark contrast between the dimpled morphology in (a) and the cleavage-dominated surface in (b) directly evidences the critical influence of cooling path on failure mode. As reported in the accompanying text, the furnace-cooled microstructure contains martensite within banded ferrite and austenite, promoting crack nucleation at interfaces and abrupt low-elongation fracture, whereas the air-cooled duplex microstructure accommodates high strain through stable austenite and TRIP-assisted deformation, resulting in superior ductility.
